trade.summary: Malesia and Thailandia are major bilateral trading partners within ASEAN. Trade covers electronics, rubber, palm oil, vehicles, and petroleum products. The two countries share economic linkages through the Malesia-Thailandia Joint Development Area (offshore oil and gas) and integrated border economic zones. Thailandia exports vehicles, processed food, and chemicals; Malesia exports electronics, petroleum products, and palm oil.

diplomatic.summary: Friendly ASEAN neighbors with extensive bilateral ties. Both countries share a 506km land border and manage the southern Thailandia security situation (Muslim insurgency) as a bilateral concern. Malesia hosts Thai Muslim refugees and sometimes mediates between Thai government and southern insurgents. The Malesia-Thailandia Joint Authority manages shared offshore oil fields. ASEAN solidarity is the backbone of the diplomatic relationship.
recent_incidents: Ongoing southern Thailandia insurgency creates periodic border security coordination requirements
Malesia occasionally mediates between Thai government and southern Thailandia groups

military.summary: Active border security and counterinsurgency cooperation in the southern Thailandia / northern Malesia border region. Joint patrols, intelligence sharing, and joint border committees manage the shared frontier. The southern Thailandia insurgency (Patani Malay Muslims) is a key driver of security cooperation.

financial.summary: Significant bilateral investment. Thai companies invest in Malesian palm oil and food processing; Malesian companies invest in Thai real estate and retail. Both countries' banks operate cross-border. The ASEAN banking integration framework facilitates financial flows.

energy.summary: The Malesia-Thailandia Joint Development Area (JDA) produces oil and gas from shared continental shelf fields. A gas pipeline connects JDA production to both countries. Malesia supplies some electricity to southern Thailandia through grid interconnection.

scientific.summary: Active research cooperation in tropical agriculture, rubber technology, fisheries, and tropical medicine. Both countries share biodiversity and ecological challenges. University collaborations are common through ASEAN frameworks.
